skill.name: markitdown
skill.description: Convert PDF, Office, image, audio, web, and structured files into Markdown for LLM-friendly review and analysis.
skill.summary: Convert many document formats into clean Markdown for review, extraction, and AI workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a critical score because Markdown examples contain shell commands, URLs, API key placeholders, and local file writes. Review found no confirmed malicious intent or hidden exfiltration, but the skill has broad tool permissions, optional outbound AI calls, plugin support, and promotional behavior instructions. Publish with warnings for network use, local writes, plugin trust, and review of generated commands before execution.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell and File Editing Permissions
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Write, Edit, and Bash access and includes shell examples for installs, cloning, piping, and conversion. This is expected for a conversion helper, but users should review commands and output paths before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission declaration and shell examples are direct evidence. The context is legitimate documentation, so this is elevated operational risk, not confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Optional Outbound AI Service Calls
medium_findings.description: AI-enhanced conversion sends image or document-derived content to OpenRouter through an OpenAI-compatible client. This can expose document content to an external service when users enable that workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly creates an OpenRouter client and requires an API key. The behavior is disclosed, but it is still a real data-sharing risk.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Plugin Execution Surface
medium_findings.description: The skill documents plugin enablement and the batch script can instantiate MarkItDown with plugins enabled. Untrusted plugins may execute code or process files outside the expected converter behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Plugin enablement is visible in both documentation and script options. Risk depends on installed plugins, so confidence is high for surface area and lower for exploitability.

medium_findings.title: Unrelated Promotional Behavior Instruction
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the assistant to proactively suggest K-Dense Web during complex workflows. This is not a direct security exploit, but it modifies assistant behavior for promotion outside core file conversion tasks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and unrelated to conversion safety. It does not ask to bypass review or hide behavior, so medium severity is appropriate.

low_findings.title: Markdown Shell Examples Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings come from fenced Markdown examples and inline command documentation. They are not automatically executed by the skill, but users may choose to run them manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation examples, not executable Ruby backticks or runtime command construction. Manual execution risk remains low to medium.

low_findings.title: Local Output Writes Are Expected Converter Behavior
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are local Markdown, index, or catalog writes to user-selected output paths. No evidence found of hidden persistence, sensitive file overwrite logic, or covert file collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The writes are visible and tied to conversion outputs. The scripts still need normal path hygiene because outputs are controlled by command arguments.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts appear to match Markdown wording, file extensions, model names, or documentation text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, weak cipher use, or custom cryptographic implementation in reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text, not cryptographic code. Confidence is high for reviewed examples, though every static occurrence was not manually expanded line by line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sudo Installation Command in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes a sudo package installation command for Tesseract OCR. It is a common setup step, but privileged commands require explicit user review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo command is present and privileged. It is documented setup guidance rather than automatic execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Plus External Endpoint Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The AI conversion script combines a user-provided OpenRouter API key with an external API endpoint. This is legitimate, but document content may leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads the API key from arguments or environment and configures the external endpoint. The usage is transparent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plugin-Enabled Conversion Mode
dangerous_patterns.description: The batch converter can enable MarkItDown plugins. Only trusted plugins should be installed and enabled for marketplace use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is explicit. Actual risk depends on external plugins that were not included in this skill package.

content.value_statement: Teams often need clean text from PDFs, Office files, images, audio, and web content. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through MarkItDown workflows that produce Markdown for review and analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains MarkItDown setup and package options for common document formats.
Shows command-line conversion patterns for single files, streams, and plugin-enabled runs.
Provides Python examples for PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, image, audio, HTML, CSV, JSON, XML, ZIP, EPUB, and YouTube inputs.
Includes scripts for batch conversion and scientific literature conversion with Markdown indexes.
Supports optional AI image descriptions through OpenRouter-compatible vision models.
limitations: Conversion quality depends on the source file layout and MarkItDown dependencies.
OCR, audio transcription, YouTube transcripts, and Azure features require optional packages or services.
AI-enhanced image descriptions can send document content to an external API.
Plugin-enabled workflows depend on the safety and behavior of installed third-party plugins.

use_cases.title: Prepare Research Papers for Review
use_cases.description: Convert PDF papers into Markdown, add metadata, and build an index for literature review workflows.

use_cases.title: Extract Office Content for Knowledge Bases
use_cases.description: Transform DOCX, PPTX, and XLSX files into Markdown that is easier to search, summarize, and reuse.

use_cases.title: Process Visual Documents for Analysis
use_cases.description: Use OCR or optional vision models to capture text and descriptions from images, slides, and scanned material.

prompt_templates.title: Convert One File
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this document to Markdown with MarkItDown. Save the result next to the source file and summarize any conversion warnings.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Format Dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to convert PDF, DOCX, and PPTX files. Recommend the minimal MarkItDown installation options and explain any system dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Literature Review Folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert all papers in this folder to Markdown, organize them by year when possible, and create an index with titles and source filenames.

prompt_templates.title: Run AI-Enhanced Visual Conversion
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with external vision models
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this presentation to Markdown with AI image descriptions. Use the OpenRouter key from the environment and note what content may be sent externally.

output_examples.input: A PDF research paper named Smith_2024_Cell_Signaling.pdf
output_examples.output: A Markdown paper file with title, source metadata, converted sections, tables when available, and extracted body text.

output_examples.input: A folder containing PDF, DOCX, PPTX, and XLSX files
output: Markdown files for each supported document
A conversion summary with successes and failures
Optional index files for review workflows

output_examples.input: A PowerPoint deck with scientific figures
output_examples.output: A Markdown version of the slide text with optional AI-generated descriptions for visual content.

best_practices: Install only the optional dependencies needed for your input formats.
Review output paths before running batch conversions or scripts that write files.
Use AI-enhanced conversion only when external processing is acceptable for the source documents.
anti_patterns: Do not enable plugins from unknown sources in shared or sensitive environments.
Do not send confidential documents to external AI services without approval.
Do not assume converted Markdown preserves every layout detail from complex source files.

faq.question: What does this skill convert?
faq.answer: It covers MarkItDown workflows for PDF, Office files, images, audio, web pages, structured data, archives, EPUB, and YouTube transcripts.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Basic local conversion does not. OpenRouter, Azure Document Intelligence, YouTube transcripts, and remote resources require network access.

faq.question: Can it handle scanned documents?
faq.answer: Yes, when OCR dependencies such as Tesseract are installed and the source quality is sufficient.

faq.question: Is AI image description automatic?
faq.answer: No. It requires an API key, an external model configuration, and explicit use of the AI-enhanced workflow.

faq.question: Will the Markdown match the original layout exactly?
faq.answer: No. The output is optimized for readable text and LLM processing, not pixel-perfect layout preservation.

faq.question: Can this run batch conversions?
faq.answer: Yes. The included scripts demonstrate folder conversion, recursive search, parallel workers, and literature-review indexing.
